nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Oh. Oh, you're real. Forgive me. I haven't seen one of you up close in... it's been a while. A long while. I'm Sola. I've been standing here long enough to remember when this field hummed. You look like you just woke up. Which is funny. Because something was supposed to wake up here. Eventually.

choices.text: Buzz curiously — what do you mean?
Buzz uncertainly — I don't remember anything.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Before this field went quiet — before the hive near the old oak stopped making sound — something was sealed away. Carefully. I always assumed it would either work or it wouldn't. Apparently it worked.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: That makes sense. The hive near the old oak went quiet two seasons ago. But before it did — something was preserved. I don't know if it was instinct or intention. You're here. So I suppose it doesn't matter which.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: The heaviness you're feeling — that's not disorientation. Things are genuinely different from when you went under. You're not misreading the world. The world changed. But you're in it. Which is more than I expected by now.

choices.text: Buzz — what happened to the others?
Buzz — why am I the one that woke up?

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Many things at once. Over a long time. No single reason — which somehow makes it harder to hold. They didn't stop coming back because they gave up. They just stopped coming back. There's a difference. I need you to know that.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: I don't know. I've thought about it. Maybe there doesn't need to be a reason. Maybe the question worth asking isn't why you — but what now. And I think you already know part of that answer. You flew straight to a flower.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: I don't know your name. Bees don't really do names, do they? That's alright. I'll just call you what you are. Worker. That's not an insult — it's about the most honorable thing I know how to say. Your whole life is built around doing something that keeps the rest of us alive. Do you actually know what that something is?

choices.text: Buzz confidently — of course I do.
Buzz uncertainly — I'm not sure anymore.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Good. Then let me say it out loud anyway — for my sake. It helps me remember why I'm still trying to bloom.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Of course you do, somewhere deep down. But I'd like to tell you anyway. Words make things real. And this needs to feel real right now.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Yes. I think that's why you're here. You've been through something. I don't know what. But you feel it, don't you? Like something important slipped out of reach. Let me put it back.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: You are a pollinator. The job sounds simple — fly from flower to flower, collect what you need. But what you leave behind when you visit us... that's the whole story. You carry pollen. Dust from one flower to another. And that transfer — that small, invisible act — is how plants reproduce. Without it, they can't make seeds. Without seeds, no fruits. No nuts. No next generation.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: The food that humans eat, the trees that give them shade, the roots that hold the soil together after rain — all of it traces back to what you do when you visit a flower. You're not just a bee. You're a thread in something enormous. Your colony understood this. Every flower they visited, every field they worked — they were keeping something alive that the world can't keep alive without you. Do you see the riverbank from here? Past the fence, just past the old drain. Yesterday there was nothing moving in that water. This morning I saw a fish. One fish. But one is how it starts.

choices.text: Buzz softly — so what happened to us?
Look around the empty field — why is it so quiet here?

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: That's the right question. And I'm not going to pretend it's easy to answer. It didn't happen at once. That's what I need you to understand. There was no single morning where everything changed. It was gradual. Like a song getting quieter, note by note, until one day you realize you can't hear it anymore.

choices.text: Buzz — what changed in the world around you?
Buzz — what changed for the bees themselves?

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: The land changed first. Fields like this one used to be wild — full of variety. Different flowers, different seasons, something always blooming. Then came the big farms. One crop, one chemical, one kind of everything. The pesticides were the worst part. Sprayed to protect the harvest — I understand that, I do — but they didn't only reach the pests. They reached you. They reached the soil I grow in. They changed things in ways that don't wash away with the next rain. And the climate shifting didn't help. My blooming season moved. Your patterns didn't move with it. Timing that used to be perfect started to miss — by days, then weeks.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Your colonies started shrinking. Not from one cause — from many, all at once. Parasites arrived that your ancestors had never encountered. Food became scarce — fewer flowers, fewer varieties, longer gaps between blooms. And then there was the competition. Other species, displaced from elsewhere, moving into territories that were already struggling. Too many pressures. Not enough resilience left to absorb them. You were never weak. The world just kept adding weight.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: The humans intensified their farming to feed more people. I don't think most of them meant harm. But the side effect was this: less space for wildflowers, less food for you, more chemicals in places where chemicals were never meant to reach. You were caught in the middle of something you didn't cause.

choices.text: Buzz — is there something even stranger going on? Something no one explains?
Buzz — I've heard enough. I want to do something.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Yes. There is. Some hives didn't just shrink. They emptied. Not from disease you could see, not from pests you could point to. The workers simply... didn't come back. Food still in the hive. Young bees still waiting. But the adults — the ones who flew out into the world — they never returned. No bodies. No explanation. Just absence. They called it Colony Collapse Disorder. A name that sounds clinical, like they understand it. They don't. Not fully. The leading theory is that something — perhaps the chemicals, perhaps the parasites, perhaps all of it together — caused the adult bees to die suddenly, out in the field, far from home. Hives that were full in the morning, empty by evening. That's what I mean when I say this isn't one problem. This is everything, at once, with no warning.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Good. That feeling — hold onto it. Some of them are making choices right now. Small ones. A bottle on the ground instead of the bin. A wrapper dropped without thinking. Those small choices add up to the world I just described. You know what to do.

nodes.speaker: flower
nodes.text: Go. Fly. Do what you were made to do. And when you find a flower — stop. Listen. We've been watching this happen for a long time. We have things to tell you. I'll be here when you come back. I'm not going anywhere. I never could.
